title: The MBTA is loving the World Cup energy, but the escalator rowing? Not so much.
author: Abby Patkin
bullets: World Cup “Adding this to the list of things I’ve never seen before and probably never will again.
” Norway fans on the train heading to "Boston Stadium" (Gillette Stadium) for the World Cup game against Iraq.

Jessica Rinaldi / The Boston Globe By Abby Patkin June 17, 2026 | 1:20 PM 1 minute to read Related: Not to be outdone by Scotland’s Tartan Army, Norway fans in town for the World Cup have charmed Bostonians with their synchronized “rowing” on escalators around the city.

title: ‘Thousands’ of Scottish fans are taking pictures in front of this Boston liquor store. Here’s why.
author: John Waller
bullets: World Cup "I saw a photo of the shop online and it was immediately top of my side-quest list.

" By John Waller June 17, 2026 | 1:00 PM 1 minute to read The Tartan Army in Boston: Seemingly all Boston bars, breweries, and liquor stores are seeing an upswing in business with the Tartan Army in town.
But one Boston packie is attracting Scottish visitors for another reason as well.

title: Scotland World Cup fans rate Regina Pizzeria: ‘This is wicked!’
author: Annie Jonas
bullets: World Cup The North End landmark earned respectable marks from two visitors — and one unforgettable jalapeño reaction.
Regina Pizzeria in the North End.

(Lane Turner/Globe Staff) By Annie Jonas June 17, 2026 | 11:55 AM 1 minute to read As World Cup visitors continue sampling Boston’s food scene, two Scottish friends visited Regina Pizzeria in the North End and came away impressed — though not entirely blown away by the experience.

title: Bars are packed for the World Cup. But are servers cashing in?
author: Beth Treffeisen
bullets: World Cup Packed bars and empty kegs have become common during the tournament, raising questions about how the influx of fans is affecting tipped workers.
Bartender Benny Hoey holds a glass of Boston lager at the downtown taproom.

John Tlumacki/Globe Staff World Cup: Before World Cup fans arrived in Massachusetts, some restaurant owners worried international visitors might not be familiar with the American tradition of tipping.

title: ‘That’s a good problem to have’: Even Boston sports radio is out of World Cup complaints

bullets: Morning Sports Update Boston's noisiest soccer critics have momentarily embraced the World Cup.
